schools.summary: Gill: a controversy between two or more brought to judgment.
Henry: the accuser and accused brought face to face before the judges, that the cause be tried.

themes: they shall justify the righteous, and condemn the wicked
the very end of justice
to justify the wicked an abomination (Prov. 17:15)

schools.summary: Gill: lesser crimes than capital ones were punished with beating or scourging, the offences set out by the Jews; the beating before the judge's face, by a certain number.

themes: the judge shall cause him... to be beaten before his face, according to his fault, by a certain number
punishment measured and witnessed
even chastisement ruled by measure (Isa. 27:8)

schools.summary: Gill: the Jewish canons fixed it at thirty-nine, 'forty save one,' that the number be not exceeded, lest the brother seem vile.

themes: Forty stripes he may give him, and not exceed... lest thy brother should seem vile unto thee
the dignity of even the guilty guarded
Paul received forty save one (2 Cor. 11:24)

schools.summary: Gill: the ox, used in treading out corn as in ploughing, might not be restrained by a muzzle from eating as it had opportunity, but was free to eat of the corn it laboured in.

themes: Thou shalt not muzzle the ox when he treadeth out the corn
mercy to the beast teaching the labourer's due
the worker worthy of his reward (1 Cor. 9:9; 1 Tim. 5:18)

schools.summary: Gill: 'brethren dwell together' means in the same house, brought up together; the brother was to take her to wife and raise up seed to the dead.

themes: her husband's brother shall... take her to him to wife
levirate marriage preserving the dead's name
the kinsman-redeemer, fulfilled in Christ (Ruth 4:10)

schools.summary: Gill: the firstborn she bore to her husband's brother.
Keil & Delitzsch: he succeeding in the name of the dead, that his name be not put out of Israel.

themes: the firstborn which she beareth shall succeed in the name of his brother which is dead, that his name be not put out of Israel
God valuing each name in his covenant
Christ shall see his seed (Isa. 53:10)

schools.summary: Gill: the provision for when the brother refused, as Onan would have done had this been law then; the widow was to go to the elders and declare his refusal.

themes: My husband's brother refuseth to raise up unto his brother a name in Israel
the widow's recourse to the elders
the helpless given a voice (Luke 18:3)

schools.summary: Gill: the elders were to require him to come, recite the law, explain it, exhort him to comply or show reason, and have his final resolution; he might stand to it.

themes: the elders of his city shall call him... and if he stand to it, and say, I like not to take her
duty urged by the elders, not forced
the will left free (Philem. 14)

schools.summary: Gill: the time and place appointed beforehand, the woman apprised, coming for the ceremony.
Clarke: the difficulty of finding the reason of these rites, marks of contempt.

themes: loose his shoe from off his foot, and spit in his face... that will not build up his brother's house
a public shame on selfish neglect
the loosed shoe a surrendered right (Ruth 4:7)

schools.summary: Gill: not his personal name but his family was so called, a mark of infamy resting on his house as well, set in Israel.

themes: his name shall be called in Israel, The house of him that hath his shoe loosed
a lasting reproach on neglect of duty
unkindness branded with reproach (Prov. 10:7)

schools.summary: Gill: the men strive and come to blows, contending for mastery. Calvin: this law, though apparently harsh, shows how pleasing modesty is to God and how he abominates indecency.

themes: the wife of the one... putteth forth her hand, and taketh him by the secrets
modesty preserved even in conflict
the end no excuse for indecency (1 Tim. 2:9)

schools.summary: Gill: done by the civil magistrate or his order, not the man or bystander; the severity to deter women from so immodest and injurious an action; our Lord is thought to refer to it.

themes: Then thou shalt cut off her hand, thine eye shall not pity her
a severe deterrent against shamelessness
what offends cut off decisively (Matt. 5:30)

schools.summary: Gill: weights were of stone, 'a stone and a stone,' and were not to be different.
Henry: the forbidding of unjust dealing by false weights.

themes: Thou shalt not have in thy bag divers weights, a great and a small
honesty in business guarded
a false balance abomination, a just weight his delight (Prov. 11:1)

schools.summary: Gill: the ephah, holding above a bushel, is put for all measures, which should be alike and not different.
Keil & Delitzsch: the ban on a great and a small measure.

themes: Thou shalt not have in thine house divers measures, a great and a small
the ban on fraud reaching from weights to measures
thorough honesty in every dealing (2 Cor. 8:21)

schools.summary: Gill: full weights and full measures, alike and according to the standard of the country; the promise that their days be lengthened in the land.

themes: thou shalt have a perfect and just weight, a perfect and just measure... that thy days may be lengthened
honesty commanded and rewarded
the just man's integrity blessed (Prov. 20:7)

schools.summary: Gill: those who keep different weights and measures and use them to defraud their neighbours in buying and selling; all who do unrighteously are an abomination to God.

themes: all that do such things, and all that do unrighteously, are an abomination unto the LORD
fraud a sin God abhors
unjust gain loathsome, riches not by right (Jer. 17:11)

schools.summary: Gill: the Amalekites came out against them and fought at Rephidim. Calvin: they were the first to make a hostile attack and interrupt their journey, and God's sentence was pronounced against them.

themes: Remember what Amalek did unto thee by the way, when ye were come forth out of Egypt
a peculiar wickedness against the redeemed
Amalek the enemy of God's church (Exod. 17:16)

schools.summary: Gill: he met them not with provisions, as humanity would, but sword in hand to stop their journey and make them captives.
Keil & Delitzsch: he smote the hindmost, the feeble and faint, when they were weary.

themes: he... smote the hindmost of thee, even all that were feeble behind thee... and he feared not God
cruelty to the weak, void of pity
the want of God's fear the root of inhumanity (Gen. 20:11)

schools.summary: Gill: when they had rest from all their enemies round about, not only the Canaanites but all neighbouring nations, they were to blot out the remembrance of Amalek and not forget.

themes: thou shalt blot out the remembrance of Amalek from under heaven; thou shalt not forget it
the implacable enemy's certain doom
the LORD's war with Amalek forever (Exod. 17:16)
